Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Understand the Goal The objective is to create a Python dictionary representing a Log Skeleton for a specific order fulfillment process scenario The dictionary must follow a specific schema equivalence always before always after never together activ freq directly follows based on the provided definitions of declarative constraints

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Scenario Context Large scale distribution center order fulfillment Activities Receive Order RO Check Availability CA Reserve Items RI Pick Items PI Quality Check QC Pack Order PO Generate Shipping Label GSL Dispatch D Invoice Customer IC Record Payment RP

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Determine Logical Relationships Process Flow Start Everything begins with Receive Order RO Availability After receiving we must Check Availability CA Reservation If available we Reserve Items RI Usually CA to RI Picking Once reserved we Pick Items PI Quality After picking we do a Quality Check QC Packing After QC passes we Pack Order PO Labeling After packing we Generate Shipping Label GSL Shipping After label we Dispatch D Billing Invoice Customer IC usually happens around dispatch or after Payment Record Payment RP happens after Invoice Customer IC

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Lets make IC happen after D for a linear flow or allow it to happen after PO Lets stick to a standard linear flow for clarity unless specified otherwise RO to CA to RI to PI to QC to PO to GSL to D IC and RP are administrative IC usually follows Dispatch RP follows IC

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Most activities happen exactly once per order In a real warehouse Pick Items might happen multiple times but for a standard skeleton model we usually assume 1 per case unless specified Lets set all to 1 for simplicity representing a standard successful order flow

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Directly Follows This is the strongest constraint A happens B immediately follows However for a skeleton representing the ideal process model we can define the main sequence here Or we can use Always Before for broader ordering and Directly Follows for tight coupling Lets use directly follows for the tight physical flow and use always before for logical dependencies that might have gaps

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually to make the skeleton robust directly follows should only be used where steps are tightly coupled without intervening tasks RO to CA Tight CA to RI Tight RI to PI Tight PI to QC Tight QC to PO Tight PO to GSL Tight GSL to D Tight D to IC maybe not direct could be automated later Lets put D to IC in always before instead to be safe or keep it direct if automated Lets assume automated D to IC is direct IC to RP Direct

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Actually the definition says If the first activity occurs then the second activity should have been executed previously Wait lets reread carefully Definition Always Before If the first activity occurs then the second activity should have been executed previously So A B in always before means B happens before A Correction Lets look at Always After If the first activity occurs then the second activity is executed in one of the following events A to B Okay I need to be very careful with the tuple order based on the prompt specific definition

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: always before A B means B happens before A always after A B means B happens after A directly follows A B means B happens immediately after A equivalence A B means Count A equals Count B never together A B means not both in case

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Re evaluating the Flow with Prompt Definitions Flow RO to CA to RI to PI to QC to PO to GSL to D to IC to RP directly follows RO CA CA RI RI PI PI QC QC PO PO GSL GSL D D IC IC RP always before if I want to say RO is before RP Tuple RP RO means If RP occurs RO was executed previously This matches the prompt definition always after if I want to say RO is before RP Tuple RO RP means If RO occurs RP is executed later

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: Business Rule You cannot Invoice IC before Dispatch D So IC D in always before Business Rule You cannot Dispatch D before Receive Order RO So D RO in always before

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Equivalence Are there activities that happen the same number of times RO and RP One order one payment record Yes PI and QC One pick one quality check Yes PO and GSL One pack one label Yes This helps ensure consistency in the log

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Never Together Since the scenario describes a standard fulfillment process without explicit alternative paths like Cancel Order never together might be empty Actually looking at the activities there are no obvious mutually exclusive activities provided in the list I will leave this set empty

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Activity Occurrences All activities should happen exactly once per order case

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Review against Prompt Constraints Python dictionary format Yes Keys match exactly Yes Values match types Yes Definitions respected always before A B means B before A always after A B means B after A directly follows A B means B immediately after A Scenario logic applied Yes

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self Correction on always before definition My logic in step 5 RP RO means If RP occurs RO was previously executed This is correct Self Correction on always after definition RO RP means If RO occurs RP happens later This is correct

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: To avoid over constraining or redundancy I will keep directly follows for the strict chain and always after for the overall case closure

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Final check on specific keys spelling equivalence always before always after never together activ freq directly follows Matches prompt exactly One final check on value types Sets containing pairs tuples Dictionary mapping activity to set of counts Matches prompt exactly
